role_summary: Nation of Islam national spokesperson from 1957 to his March 1964 break with the organization; founder of the Muslim Mosque Inc and the Organization of Afro-American Unity (OAAU) in 1964; assassinated February 21, 1965.
ai_reading: The precomputed who-discusses-whom influence graph (/rag/summaries/influence.json) registers Malcolm X as discussed by five interviewees: William Lamar Strickland (entry 127), Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34, the SNCC Washington office director who edited Carmichael's memoir), John Carlos (entry 62, the 1968 Olympic Black Power salute), John Churchville (entry 63), and William S. Leventhal (entry 129). That graph-derived cluster reads tightly Black Power and athlete-activist, and it does not surface the SCLC or NAACP transcripts the catalog separately tracks. The observation worth flagging is the gap between that tally and the wider corpus: a verbatim search of the transcripts finds Malcolm X named in dozens of interviews, with first-person encounter accounts from voices the influence graph does not connect to him, including Courtland Cox (entry 24), Gloria Hayes Richardson (entry 49), and D'Army Bailey (entry 26). The graph captures the figures who frame their own politics through him; it misses the larger set who simply met him, booked him, or watched a crowd gather to a recording of his voice, which the Voices from the Archive section below restores.
biographical_paragraph: Malcolm X is discussed in this corpus by 5 interviewees across the catalog (William Lamar Strickland (entry 127), Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34), John Carlos (entry 62), John Churchville (entry 63), William S. Leventhal (entry 129)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Malcolm X as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Courtland Cox
interview_snippets.lead_in: Courtland Cox, then a Howard University student in the Nonviolent Action Group, recalls a dinner before the 1961 Howard debate between Malcolm X and Bayard Rustin, moderated by government professor Emmett Dorsey:
interview_snippets.quote: And Malcolm said, Professor, I think we better let you speak tonight, because you have much more information than anybody in this room, which was really true.

interview_snippets.speaker: D'Army Bailey
interview_snippets.lead_in: D'Army Bailey, then a student at Clark University in Worcester, Massachusetts, describes arranging for Malcolm X to speak on campus:
interview_snippets.quote: Now when I was in Worcester, now when I brought Malcolm X to speak, I had called Malcolm at his home in New York and asked if he would come and speak on the campus, which he agreed to do.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Hayes Richardson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gloria Hayes Richardson, leader of the Cambridge, Maryland movement, recounts a Nation of Islam emissary relaying a message from Elijah Muhammad after she had appeared on stage with Malcolm X:
interview_snippets.quote: He came to me and told me the Honorable Elijah Muhammad, my Hamid, told me to tell you, you need to stay off of the stage with Malcolm X.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons
interview_snippets.lead_in: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons, a SNCC field secretary running the Laurel, Mississippi project, describes using a recording of Malcolm X to draw residents to voter-registration gatherings:
interview_snippets.quote: But we noticed if you played, you know, somebody like Malcolm, the people would just come out and listen.

interview_snippets.speaker: Evans Derrell Hopkins
interview_snippets.lead_in: Evans Derrell Hopkins, a Black Panther Party community worker, traces the party's origin to Malcolm X:
interview_snippets.quote: Well, yes, the party was founded just after the assassination of Malcolm X. And at one time, I think Bobby Seale and Hughie Newton even thought about calling it the Sons of Malcolm.

sources.title: Manning Marable, Malcolm X: A Life of Reinvention
